title: An Ode to Yui sama Kinda
display_title: An Ode to Yui sama Kinda
wikitext:
[]: An Ode to Yui-sama (Kinda)             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Feb 12, 2008 1:34 pm This was a discussion on IMDB, and I thought I'd relay this particular post here. Opinions welcome: In response to: stanleycoleman wrote: I recently started watching the series again for the first time in years because I forgot most of the storyline. While Yui is often portrayed as this positive loving maternal figure, she also seemed like a mad scientist with crazed ideals which started everything in the first place. So could it be said she was evil?

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Tue Feb 12, 2008 2:23 pm Wow, that is the best read of Yui I have ever seen. I too have noticed even she made mistakes over the corse of the series, tis human nature after all. I mean she could have left a video or something for Gendo and Shinji explaining her actions. I wouldn't say she is naive, I think idealistic is a much better discription.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Feb 12, 2008 2:46 pm My response was relatively simple (I also agree at the quality of the post): "I think Yui was put in the classic "rock and a hard place" position where she couldn't sit back and simply let Seele and Gendo pursue their plans unimpeded, nor could she allow the Angels to wipe them out. So she did all she could do with the resources she had, and that choice would also allow Shinji the ultimate decision of living in the world he wanted."

[]: Re: An Ode to Yui-sama (Kinda)             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Tue Feb 12, 2008 8:42 pm lee-chow wrote: I would like to open up with the comment that "evil" is a four letter word, like "love" and everyone's favorite sentence enhancer. And like "love", "evil" isn't a word one should take lightly and must be willing to take in the responsibilities in saying, especially in the Evangelion universe. I think one major point about Evangelion is how that there is no true or pure "evil", just mainly a lot of suffering due to loneliness, fear, and anger. Though it's indeed there, it persists in all individuals - especially in the main characters. No one was innocent in Evangelion; and even with what we have seen with Yui as a pure, loving mother in the series, I think that even she was not immune to this. Though she was portrayed as a saint in the show (perhaps even a true angel in the traditional sense), one must not forget that she was a human being like all the other characters - just a seldom seen one. It is the fact that we can't know what she is thinking that brings the real possibility that her intentions may not be so clear-cut. She married Gendo after all! It's this quality about her that I love and I find that makes her stand out from the other characters: her mysterious motives and intentions. On topic and to the point: while I do not doubt that our exalted, pure Yui does have a dark side, I do not believe that she was intentionally "evil". If you ask me she was genuinely hopeful, flawed in the fact that she was genuinely naive. If you were to enter an advanced field of science (like physics or biology or what have you) you would eventually come to the realization as to how limited life is and how, eventually, all things will parish: even the most basic building blocks of matter are not immune to destruction. Nothingness seems to be the inevitable result of the decay of life and energy. And yet, Yui goes on to say things along the lines of "Those who have the will to live will return" and "Even if the world comes to an end, Eva will live on as a testament of mankind's existence". As I am sure Aidan here can sense, Yui seems to hold on to this "hope" that through will power people can become happy, as if "The right to be happy is a right given to all living things". Thus arousing the suspicion on this ideal. Back to my analogy, even in a space of nothingness, within a span of eternity, there is a chance (a "hope") that matter will be reborn anew. You see, there seems to be a strange side-effect for some people when they gain a wealth of knowledge: they gain the means to effect life so that "hope" becomes reality. Given her possible naivety, her opposition to SEELE's plans and SEELE's ruthless and secretive tactics, I can easily imagine a scenario where Yui found herself backed into a corner and threatened with assassination due to her beliefs. With no one to else to help her and no where else to go, she may have had no choice but to play a gamble and give herself to the Eva she had worked to create. Thus it may have been better for her to be absorbed into the Eva than to be with her family and be murdered the next day. And given her seemingly unconditionally loving nature, she may have wanted to be alone in her endeavor. Notice that in formulating her plans, not once did Yui specifically entrust responsibility to her own husband Gendo, the one who in theory she should have been able to confide with. Perhaps she was banking on him to care for Shinji while she was gone. Instead, she went to Fuyusuki, an individual who was perhaps not as close to her as Gendo. Also notice how these same men ended up being key people in SEELE's plans for Instrumentality later on. Yui may have wanted to protect her friends and family by keeping them mostly out her plans and thus, out of the sight of SEELE. By keeping them out of harm's way by being the sole member of a massive coup, Yui spared her family and friends from SEELE's retaliation. This apparently worked, as SEELE eventually trusted her professor and her widowed husband enough to place them in jobs of high importance to their plans. And by being bonded within the shell of Eva Unit-01, she may have allowed herself to be doomed to a lonely, eternal existence because it was her way of showing remorse and penance for what had happened because of SEELE's and her actions. I don't think that she was blind to the suffering of many people as a result of the course of events, nor was she totally oblivious to what had happened to her family after her departure. She may have felt things would be set straight by enduring the greatest punishment, eternal solitude. And yet, in all of this sacrifice for the greater good, she neglects and hurts the people she leaves behind. In her push to take the greatest bulk of the work, burden, and punishment for the sake of all people, the people who need her waste away (perhaps most noteworthy of all, her own son Shinji). I can say all of this because I think I can relate to this kind of thinking: if I were given the power to save all who were dear to me (and potentially everyone on Earth), I would want to be able to lift most, if not all, the burden and suffer most, if not all, the consequences for their sake. After all, what is one life compared to many? And yet, I cannot perceive what may happen to those who actually need me to be there by their side. So you see, despite the fact that she is seen so little, there is so much going on about Yui. And like all the other characters in Eva, there is a lot underneath her surface that remains untapped.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Tue Feb 12, 2008 9:23 pm That was so well written I thought I was reading something by Yojimbo or Shin-Seiki at first! Anyway, that was rather refreshing. Although I personally don't think Yui was naive. Just very...idealistic. (If that's even a word.)

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Tue Feb 12, 2008 9:32 pm Yes, idealistic is a word. An adjective. I wonder if this person goes by any handle we know of in the usual places.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Feb 13, 2008 11:25 am UrsusArctos wrote: I wonder if this person goes by any handle we know of in the usual places.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Wed Feb 13, 2008 8:10 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: I think he's doing an excellent job already.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Feb 14, 2008 2:18 am Themaninblack wrote: Ditto...put him on the COMMENTARY!!!

[]:             Quote Post by Themaninblack » Thu Feb 14, 2008 11:17 am Mr. Tines wrote: Themaninblack wrote: Ditto...put him on the COMMENTARY!!!

[]:             Quote Post by Themaninblack » Thu Feb 14, 2008 11:17 am Mr. Tines wrote: Themaninblack wrote: Ditto...put him on the COMMENTARY!!!

[]:             Quote Post by mrpanda » Thu Feb 14, 2008 1:59 pm That was very well thought out and i agree with it totally. Any chance of that artical having a place somewhere in the wiki, possibly the theory and analysis pages ??

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Feb 14, 2008 2:35 pm Mr. Tines wrote: It's, what, 2 years since we last had a commentary episode...

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Feb 14, 2008 2:58 pm His ode to Yui, pt. II lee-chow wrote: I see, idealistic would be a better term to describe Yui. I'll switch to that. In line with the "Yui is 'evil'" train of thought, I noticed that the idea that Yui may have had a god complex was thrown around in EvaGeeks and possibly in the minds of others here on this board. Certainly this is a possibility: after all Yui was the one who was key to the Eva's development and allowed herself to be absorbed into Unit-01, thus obtaining all of its potential to alter the entire world - to become god. Her presence in it was certainly a factor, as it was widely believed that she was the one responsible for Unit-01's many savage berserk rampages. In the end, she came away from EoE inside Unit-01 in all of its terrible glory, off to act as a final testament of humanity's existence and possibly as a new seed of life. I think that it's clear that such actions (and their results) could be seen as a complex and deliberate scheme to obtain god-like power. And it's quite clear that the ends (to stop SEELE) were intended by the doctor. But were the means (everything else) intended? Looking back at what little we see of her in the show, it should be noticeable as to how simple of a person Yui was. She was depicted as a very soft-spoken, down-to-earth, and kind human being. Even her character design was very simple: she wasn't a caricature, she didn't have any special features, hair color, or scars like the other women on the show. Her only distinctive feature was her resemblance to Rei. Other than that she should have been a forgettable character. She was just a woman, and a mother at that. Also, this was the same woman who said to her professor that what she really wanted to do after college wasn't to enter some big research project, but to start a family. Also note that it has been said that Yui was the daughter of a member of SEELE (which member only Anno knows) and through that connection was able to work her way into the organization's top projects. It can also be implied that Yui was from a fairly wealthy and upper-class heritage: as she was able to obtain an education in one of Japan's highest institutions (perhaps THE highest, since Tokyo University was destroyed along with the rest of Tokyo I) and, if you recall, Gendo took HER name when they married (there's some unwritten tradition in Japan that if a man marries a woman of a higher class, he takes her family’s name). And yet in spite of this, Yui doesn't seem to be living in any particularly lavish life style nor the rest of her family. She could have easily been mistaken for any other middle-class citizen (and Gendo still had to do some shady deals to get a hefty sum). Now pit this kind of person against the ruthless might of SEELE, an organization that has been active since the Middle Ages and has reigned on Earth unopposed since the mid-20th century. Brilliant scientist or not, what could she have done? And could anyone else have done better? The way I see it, Yui may have just been an otherwise normal woman, who only wanted the basic joys found in having a family. She could have had just about anything else (including her human life) and what she chose was a peaceful life with her husband and son. And so perhaps one of Evangelion's many tragedies was the fact that Yui was denied this one, simple wish in the end. She seems very humble and fairly unambitious - as a matter of fact I would believe that she would protest of being called "Yui-sama" as some fans call her. Such an honorific of high respect may be flattering, but I doubt that Yui would find herself to be special enough to keep it. If anything, a "normal" life was probably what was missing in her existence up until the point she met Gendo. What kind of life can you expect from being the offspring of a SEELE member (with no mention of a mother) and working on a ground breaking project like the Evas in your twenties? Being forced to give up her family life was no doubt a hard decision. To have no one else to rely on in an impossible effort must have been devastating. To see that the family that she wanted so dearly decay into an abyss of animosity must have been heart-wrenching. To sacrifice her body in exchange for the form of a god-like entity, watch the people of Earth degrade into LCL with no guarantee that they will ever restore themselves, and to drift for what may be forever into the cold vastness of space with nothing but the instruments of Third Impact and her own thoughts must have been a living nightmare. Do not kid yourselves; I doubt that there is anything in this world that could prepare anyone for this kind of fate. Of course, the idea that Yui was a simple woman can not rule out the possibility that she may still have wanted to become a god: one can be simply diabolical, or by having a simplistic worldview, one could try to manipulate those simple variables and control the fate of the world. We may never know until we see more or learn more about Yui, perhaps in the Rebuild movies. Or, knowing Anno, perhaps not...

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Feb 14, 2008 8:30 pm Excellent again, but I have to disagree with certain things. There's no doubt that Yui is upper class, but I do take the CI information(She's the daughter of a Seele member) with a grain of salt. I have to find a way to work around this one, because her attitude and worldview are so radically different from Seele's. And no mention of a mother doesn't necessarily mean most of her life was without a mother. Her dad would get a mention as a Seele member, but her mom would be a normal enough person, so why bother talking about her? I don't think this is a point. It's actually questionable if her lifestyle was that down-to-earth. Fuyutsuki goes all the way up to Gendo and points out how extravagant his spending is- Yui is perfectly normal and Shinji's a baby at that point. She could easily have had a more luxurious life than normal. And it's possible to live in luxury but still be idealistic-it's a deeply personal thing, in the end. I agree that she does seem to be a very pleasant and down-to-earth lady from what we know of her. What we really haven't seen: the big blank zone in case of Yui, and, to an extent, Fuyutsuki, is their dark side. Fuyutsuki seems to have been rather lonely in love and socially separated, although he's very altruistic. What is Yui's dark side? What is her suffering?

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Feb 14, 2008 8:33 pm Perhaps she's more like Gendou and Shinji then we see? "Everybody hates me/I'm not used to being liked.", etc Like attracts like, after all. /Deep thoughts

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Thu Feb 14, 2008 9:19 pm UrsusArctos wrote: There's no doubt that Yui is upper class, but I do take the CI information(She's the daughter of a Seele member) with a grain of salt. I have to find a way to work around this one, because her attitude and worldview are so radically different from Seele's.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Feb 14, 2008 10:31 pm Well, I'm working around it. See my "Rebuild of Yui" Fanfic for details. (Lol Rebuild)

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Fri Feb 15, 2008 12:26 am 1 word for you, DENIAL.

To sacrifice her body in exchange for the form of a god-like entity, watch the people of Earth degrade into LCL with no guarantee that they will ever restore themselves, and to drift for what may be forever into the cold vastness of space with nothing but the instruments of Third Impact and her own thoughts must have been a living nightmare. Do not kid yourselves; I doubt that there is anything in this world that could prepare anyone for this kind of fate. Of course, the idea that Yui was a simple woman can not rule out the possibility that she may still have wanted to become a god: one can be simply diabolical, or by having a simplistic worldview, one could try to manipulate those simple variables and control

Article: An Ode to Yui sama Kinda
 idea that Yui was a simple woman can not rule out the possibility that she may still have wanted to become a god: one can be simply diabolical, or by having a simplistic worldview, one could try to manipulate those simple variables and control the fate of the world. We may never know until we see more or learn more about Yui, perhaps in the Rebuild movies. Or, knowing Anno, perhaps not...

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Feb 14, 2008 8:30 pm Excellent again, but I have to disagree with certain things. There's no doubt that Yui is upper class, but I do take the CI information(She's the daughter of a Seele member) with a grain of salt. I have to find a way to work around this one, because her attitude and worldview are so radically different from Seele's. And no mention of a mother doesn't necessarily mean most of her life was without a mother. Her dad would get a mention as a Seele member, but her mom would be a normal enough person, so why bother talking about her? I don't think this is a point. It's actually questionable if her lifestyle was that down-to-earth. Fuyutsuki goes all the way up to Gendo and points out how extravagant his spending is- Yui is perfectly normal and Shinji's a baby at that point. She could easily have had a more luxurious life than normal. And it's possible to live in luxury but still be idealistic-it's a deeply personal thing, in the end. I agree that she does seem to be a very pleasant and down-to-earth lady from what we know of her. What we really haven't seen: the big blank zone in case of Yui, and, to an extent, Fuyutsuki, is their dark side. Fuyutsuki seems to have been rather lonely in love and socially separated, although he's very altruistic. What is Yui's dark side? What is her suffering?
